data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares: Preferred Shares Provide Attractive Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Capital Bancshares (NASDAQ:TCBI) (OTCPK:TCBLL) is a regional bank with locations in Texas’ five largest metropolitan areas. The bank also offers a preferred share (NASDAQ:TCBIO), which I have covered a few times, most recently back in July when the bank’s preferred shares rallied to create an unattractive yield. Today, the preferred shares have not rallied commensurate with the market’s movements, and the yield on the bank’s preferred shares now represents an attractive entry point for income investors.

Texas Capital Earnings Report

Like all banks, Texas Capital is dealing with the challenges associated with rapidly higher interest rates, followed by those rates staying higher for longer than anyone could predict. In the case of Texas Capital, asset yields rose from under 2.5% to near 6%, but borrowing yields rose even more, going from 0.5% up to near 5%. The drop in net interest spread (asset yield less borrowing yield) placed pressure on the bank’s earnings, but in the last two quarters, borrowing yields have dropped, and net interest spreads have risen. Additionally, the bank has managed leverage well during this time as the net interest margin remains well above pandemic levels.

From an income and expense standpoint, Texas Capital Bancshares had a great third quarter. Interest income jumped, while interest expense rose marginally. Net interest income (interest income less interest expense) had been on a steady decline during the past six quarters but staged a stunning upside reversal in the third quarter, nearly matching the highs achieved in late 2022. Needless to say, the prospect of lower interest rates appears to be benefitting Texas Capital’s net interest income.

Loan and Deposit Growth

A large part of the reason behind Texas Capital Bancshares’ successful third quarter lies in the bank’s ability to grow its deposits. During the quarter, Texas Capital grew deposits by more than 8.5%, vastly outperforming its peers which saw an 1.2% deposit increase in the commercial banking sector. Additionally, while loan growth has been robust over the last year, loan balances remained relatively flat during the third quarter.

Because of the deposit growth and muted loan growth, the bank’s loan to deposit ratio dropped noticeably in the third quarter to 83%. A lower loan to deposit ratio indicates a decreased need for the bank to fund its loan growth through external borrowings. In fact, Texas Capital has applied much of its deposit growth towards paying down external debt, with around 25% of its short-term and long-term debt being paid down in the last quarter. These declines in debt have allowed the bank to improve its net interest income and consequently, its earnings.

Risks to Texas Capital Bancshares

Loan performance remains a primary concern for Texas Capital Bancshares. Investors should also be mindful that the company is geographically concentrated in Texas, where swings in the local economy could affect the bank. Texas Capital does most of its lending to commercial entities, although mortgage financing is the fastest growing loan segment with the bank.

While the bank is paring back its exposure to commercial real estate loans, Texas Capital still has $5.3 billion of its $22 billion loan portfolio tied up there. Additionally, nonaccrual loans are slowly beginning to climb, reaching $89 million from $63 million a year ago. While these levels are relatively tame, the bank’s allowance for credit losses is standing at just 1.2% of gross loans, which can create earnings headwinds if the bank decides they need to build their allowance further.

Conclusion

Texas Capital Bancshares showed a great improvement in net interest income in the third quarter, thanks to a build in deposits and pay down of external debt. The bank is facing some headwinds when it comes to loan performance, but the preferred shares have very little risk of being impaired. The recent price movement in the markets has brought the preferred share yield on Texas Capital’s preferred shares up to near the top quartile of all bank preferred shares. I believe this represents a good opportunity and entry point for income investors.

data.title: Proceed With Caution: Factors To Consider Before Buying Dutch Bros Stock
data.description:
Dutch Bros (NYSE: BROS) is a popular coffee and beverage store that is relatively early in its journey from a regional chain to a national one. You can think of Dutch Bros as an early-stage Dunkin' Donuts or Starbucks (SBUX). The concept has substantial room for expansion across the country, potentially leading to significant revenue growth. The company started in 1992 in Grants Pass, Oregon, and is amid an expansion from the west coast southeastward. The company's long-term forecast is for 4,000+ shops within 10 to 15 years. Management targets a mid-teen annual percentage growth rate in store openings over that period. By the end of the second quarter of 2024, Dutch Bros reached 912 shops.

The following map shows the company's footprint at the end of 2023.

Investing in restaurant concepts early in their regional to national expansion can be very lucrative. An example of how profitable regional-to-national restaurant stories can become is my recommendation of Wingstop (WING) On September 11, 2020. As I write this on October 18, 2024, that recommendation produced a total return of 201.11%, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) rise of 73.67% over the same period. However, unlike my recent recommendation of Portillo's (PTLO), another regional-to-national restaurant concept, the market may have already priced in the Dutch Bros expansion over the next 10 to 15 years.

This article will discuss Dutch Bros shops' operation and business fundamentals. It will also review the company's valuation, risks, and why the stock is a Hold at its current valuation.

Dutch Bros shops

Although Dutch Bros is similar to Starbucks in that it's primarily a beverage company, it differs because Dutch Bros uses a drive-thru business model where it conducts 90% of its business in a drive-thru setting. The other 10% consists of sales through a walk-up window. It has no sit-down accommodations in its stores. The company's 2023 10-K states:

Our shops typically have a smaller footprint than other drive-thru formats - this compact footprint allows us greater flexibility to place locations on more parcels. We typically target lots that are at least 25,000 square feet to handle substantial car volume. Except for a handful of legacy "coffee houses", all our shops deploy either a single or double drive-thru window with multiple feeder lanes for traffic flow.

The company has perfected the process of processing orders in its drive-thru business. The following image shows how management has set up the typical drive-thru.

Customers enter in a double lane that merges into one. The company equips employees named runners with tablets to greet cars in both lanes and take orders. Drinks are prepared within the stores by Broistas, which is what the company calls its baristas. Once the Broista finishes the drinks, the runners take them to customers in their cars. Some customers receive their drinks directly through the drive-thru window. Once customers receive their order, they can use an escape lane to get out of line and leave the drive-thru.

Dutch Bros has also started rolling out mobile orders, reaching approximately 200 shops by the end of July. Management hopes to achieve mobile orders in most of its stores by the end of 2024 and for it to be a growth driver in 2025. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Christine Barone said on the company's second quarter 2024 earnings call:

Many of our shops have double drive-thru setups that include an escape plane, which could be utilized for mobile order. After checking in, our mobile order customers can be directed to the right-hand lane, where a runner can bring their drink if it is ready before the customer reaches the window. The customer can then escape using that open lane to their right, eliminating a potential bottleneck at the window. We already use this operational tactic in many of our locations.

Besides people liking the store's custom-ordered drinks, customers enjoy the fast and friendly customer service.

Although Dutch Bros drinks can serve hot drinks, the company specializes in iced beverages. It serves seasonal drinks, specialty drinks, energy drinks, iced tea, lemonade, chai latte, hot chocolate, shakes, smoothies, and sparkling soda. The company allows customers to customize their drinks, and it even sells a few snacks to go with the drinks.

The company's growth strategy is to open stores in areas where its drinks would likely be in heavy demand. Dutch Bros's 2023 10-K states its growth strategy:

"Leverage strong unit-level economics, ample liquidity, and robust underwriting standards to open shops with attractive returns, balancing building types, lease financing structure, and market entry strategies as needed."

Company fundamentals

Dutch Bros grew company-operated stores by 29% and total stores by 21% year over year in the second quarter of 2024. Investors should expect the company to produce a year-over-year store expansion above 15% consistently. If new store growth drops below 15%, something may be wrong with its regional-to-national growth expansion strategy. CEO Barone said in the second quarter 2024 earnings release:

"We added 36 shops in the second quarter, marking the 12th consecutive quarter of 30 or more new shop openings."

Investors should also watch the company's expansion into new states. In the first quarter, it became a coast-to-coast brand when it opened three new stores in Orlando, Florida, and one in Daytona Beach. Also, pay attention to the company's reception by customers when entering new markets. CEO Barone said on the first quarter earnings call:

"Our first Florida shop, which are 3,000 miles away from our original start in Grants Pass, Oregon, have been met with enthusiastic customer demand, a testament to the strength of our brand."

Another vital metric is the company's same-store sales ("SSS"), which measures its ability to retain and attract new customers. Dutch Bros produced a second-quarter 2024 SSS of 5.2% for company-operated stores and 4.1% for systemwide sales, outpacing the current annual inflation rate of 2.4%. If Dutch Bros can maintain an SSS of 4.0% or higher, it can increase revenue growth.

The above chart shows that, on average, it takes Dutch Bros' new company-operated store locations one quarter to produce positive gross margins. After four quarters, the new stores' average gross profit margin reached 24%. This metric shows that management can quickly ramp up new store profitability, indicating potential strong returns over the long term if it can maintain its new store opening growth rate and speed to produce positive gross margins.

Management emphasizes company-operated metrics in its investor presentation because they account for most of its revenue and revenue growth. Dutch Bros grew its second-quarter 2024 total revenue by 30% year over year to $325 million and its second-quarter company-operated revenue by 34% to $295 million.

Dutch Bros' second quarter 2024 company-operated shop contribution margin, the portion of revenue that covers fixed costs and generates a profit, was up 50 basis points ("bps") year over year to 30.8%.

Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Josh Guenser said on the second quarter 2024 earnings call:

We delivered outstanding financial results in Q2 and thank our entire team for their great efforts. Once again, our performance was headlined by a combination of strong revenue growth, which was driven by positive same-shop sales growth and increasing new shop productivity alongside strong margin flow through.

The company's second-quarter 2024 adjusted EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) margin rose 70 bps to 20.1%. Management uses EBITDA because the number removes many one-time and non-cash expenses that it generates from quarter to quarter to provide better clarity of Dutch Bros' core profitability. The company is relatively new to the market and occasionally generates non-recurring expenses for which it may have difficulty giving guidance. The following table is the company's reconciliation from net income to adjusted EBITDA.

Although Dutch Bros is currently GAAP profitable, it gives guidance for adjusted EBITDA, not earnings-per-share ("EPS") or net income. As a result, the company may miss analysts' EPS forecasts, but it still may meet company guidance for adjusted EBITDA. The company generated second-quarter GAAP EPS of $0.12, missing analysts' estimates by $0.05.

One last vital metric to follow is Average Unit Volumes ("AUVs"), which measure the average sales from each Dutch Bros shop over a trailing 12-month ("TTM") period. Dutch Bros management and its potential new franchisees use AUVs to gauge the potential sales of mature shop locations. Investors loosely use this number to value the chain as a whole. For instance, the market may award a higher valuation to a restaurant company with a higher AUV than its competitors.

Dutch Bros's TTM cash flow from operation ("CFO") to sales is 17.41%, and its CFO is $194.80 million. Notice how these metrics collapsed right around the time inflation peaked in mid-2022. Now that inflation has trended down, CFO-to-sales has risen higher.

Dutch Bros TTM capital expenditures ("CapEx) in the second quarter was $248.06, reflecting the costs to construct and equip new stores. The faster the company grows, the higher the CapEx. As the business scales, it should eventually achieve positive free cash flow ("FCF"). However, investors should not expect the company to achieve positive FCF soon. Dutch Bros produced a TTM FCF loss of $53.26 in the second quarter.

Dutch Bros ended its June quarter with $260.92 million in cash and short-term investments and $229 million in long-term debt. The company has a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.79, indicating it finances its operations using debt more than equity. It has a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 4.11, which is relatively high. Investors may become concerned with its debt position if this metric continues to rise. Dutch Bros' cash position helps partially offset the risk of a high debt load. However, since the company has a negative FCF, it may have to raise capital sometime in the future to continue funding its store expansion.

Management liked its second-quarter performance so much that it raised its revenue guidance from between $1.20 billion and $1.215 billion to between $1.215 billion and $1.230 billion while guiding for 2024 full-year adjusted EBITDA between $200 and $210 million, a rise from first quarter guidance of between $195 million and $205 million.

If the company hits the midpoint of its annual revenue guidance, it will produce 26.58% year-over-year growth, continuing a deceleration from 30.68% annual revenue growth in the previous year.

If the company hits the mid-point of its adjusted EBITDA guidance and revenue guidance, its implied 2024 annual EBITDA margin would be 16.73%, up slightly from 2023's adjusted EBITDA margin of 16.57%.

Valuation

Dutch Bros' forward enterprise value (EV)-to-EBITDA is 23.16, over double the consumer discretionary sector median of 10.34, a sign of an overvalued stock.

Dutch Bros' PS ratio is 2.50, above the three-year median of 2.359. Some might consider the stock fairly valued or slightly overvalued.

The following compares Dutch Bros' one-year forward P/S with two other fast-casual restaurants, Sweetgreen (SG) and Shake Shack (SHAK), each with a market cap of around $4 billion. Dutch Bros has the lowest valuation yet the fastest revenue growth. However, it has the lowest AUV, which may explain why it has the lowest valuation in this comparison. So, the market may not necessarily undervalue Dutch Bros based on this relative valuation.

Company Name One-year forward P/S ratio Past Annual Revenue growth Expected one-year forward Annual Growth Gross margin TTM TTM AUV
Sweetgreen 5.49 24.24% 16.14% 19.10% $2.9 million
Shake Shack 3.06 20.77% 14.65% 44.78% $3.75 million *
Dutch Bros 2.68 30.68% 20.40% 26.49% $1.9 million

* I could not find Shake Shack's AUV in its financial documents, so I calculated the metric by dividing TTM company-owned sales of $1169 million by 311 company-owned stores, which equals an AUV of $3.75 million.

Dutch Bros' one-year forward PEG ratio is 3.19 (One-year forward price-to-earnings of 71.51 divided by analysts' estimated 2025 EPS growth of 22.37%). Growth investors generally consider a PEG above 2.0 as a sign of an overvalued stock.

Suppose Dutch Bros sold at a one-year forward PEG ratio of 2.0; its stock price would be $21.92, down 59.76% from its October 18 closing price of $35.02.

The company looks overvalued based on most traditional valuation methods. Seeking Alpha Quant rates the valuation an F.

Risks

Dutch Bros stock has been rather lackluster in 2024, up only 9.5% year-to-date as of October 17, 2024, compared to the S&P's rise of around 22.5%. Investors may have been unenthusiastic about the company in 2024 because of the high amount of insider selling. The following chart shows insider selling spiked much higher in 2024.

Alternatively, fewer insiders bought shares, and what they did buy was less than what they sold. Over the last six months, insiders' net shares sold were 2,576,552. Included in the insider selling was Executive Chairman of the Board Travis Boersma, who sold Dutch Bros shares as part of a 10b5-1 trading plan on several occasions in September. A 10b5-1 trading plan helps prevent an insider from making stock sales based on non-public information. However, when insiders sell significantly more than they are buying, sometimes investors interpret the selling as insiders believing the market overvalues the stock, making investors hesitant to buy it.

Restaurants are especially susceptible to inflation. The following image shows the costs subtracted from Dutch Bros' company-operated shop results. Note that Beverage, Food and Packaging, and Labor Costs are the most significant expenses. When labor and commodity costs rise, the company's shop gross profits and contribution margin suffer.

Dutch Bros came public just as the inflation rate began rising in 2021, ultimately peaking at 9.1% in June 2022. The company also doesn't do well in a rising interest rate environment, as it negatively impacts its ability to finance growth and potentially reduces consumer demand. The following chart shows the company's stock price collapsed right around peak inflation and has yet to recover to $36.92, the price at the end of its first day of trading. People who bought this stock before June 2022 are down substantially. When investing in restaurants, especially smaller restaurant concepts in growth mode, pay close attention to the inflation rate. For instance, should inflation spike again and you see Dutch Bros' labor and commodity costs spike, be wary of using a "buy the dip" strategy until inflationary pressures ease.

Dutch Bros is in a highly competitive industry. Companies like Starbucks and Dunkin' Donuts won't sit idly while Dutch Bros whittles away its market share. Dunkin' Donuts has 9,707 stores in the U.S. market, and Starbucks has 16,482 stores across the U.S. Both companies have more prominent brand names and financial resources. The company faces other regional and local competitors, such as San Francisco-based Philz Coffee, national chain Peet's Coffee, a primarily East Coast chain named La Colombe, California-based chain The Coffee Bean & Tea Leaf, and hundreds of independently owned coffee shops. Dutch Bros must continue establishing its brand and innovating in the beverages it serves to maintain or grow its market share.

Dutch Bros business model's expansion plans are vulnerable to a limited number of prime drive-thru locations. The company's second-quarter 10-Q states:

There is no guarantee that a sufficient number of suitable sites for shops will be available in desirable areas or on terms that are acceptable to us in order to achieve our growth plan. If we are unable to open new shops, or if existing franchise partners do not open new shops, or if shop openings are significantly delayed, our revenue or earnings growth could be adversely affected and our business may be harmed.

The Wall Street Journal wrote in 2022, "Demand for the humble drive-through lane is booming, as coffee makers, chicken joints and other fast-food operators vie for limited supply." Starbucks is actively moving into the drive-thru space. Then Starbucks CEO Howard Schultz said on the company's second quarter 2022 earnings call, "We will also be accelerating our new store growth with 90% of new stores being high-returning drive-thru." Starbucks isn't the only popular restaurant turning to drive-thru; Chick-fil-A, McDonald's (MCD), Dunkin, and more utilize this drive-thru strategy.

Another issue is that more and more cities are banning drive-thrus to combat traffic congestion, limiting adequate sites. So before investors price in 4,000 stores into the company's valuation, they should consider the risk that Dutch Bros may be unable to achieve that U.S. store count. Investors must closely monitor the company's store growth rate. The stock may sell off if new store openings sink below 15%.

Dutch Bros is a hold

The following chart shows inflationary pressures have eased, and the Federal Reserve has made its first interest rate cuts. The headwinds that the company has endured over the last two years may be turning into tailwinds.

Dutch Bros is coming off a strong second quarter, and the fundamentals behind its regional-to-national growth strategy are solid. There are excellent reasons investors have bought into this company. However, the stock sells at a very high valuation, is FCF negative, has high debt, and is susceptible to execution issues. Although the stock may not necessarily drop from current prices due to potential growth into its current valuation over time, investors may become disappointed by stagnant price appreciation. Aswath Damodaran once said, "After all, the greatest company in the world with superb managers can be a bad investment, if it is priced too high." Dutch Bros may fall into the "great company, bad investment" category.

I place this stock in the neutral category, meaning I am not bullish or bearish. The company has significant revenue- and earnings-generating potential over time, but several risks prevent me from recommending a buy at today's valuation. Therefore, I give it a Hold recommendation.

data.title: Taking Stock Of Apple As Earnings Near
data.description:
With a market capitalization of nearly $3.6 trillion, Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) is the largest publicly traded company on the planet at this moment. It truly is a testament to the power of creative thought and the application of that creativity to technology. In the long run, I would imagine that further growth for the business is likely, even though financial results in recent years have not been terribly impressive. The most recent figures provided by management, which involve the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, do show that most parts of the company are growing at this moment. However, this doesn't mean that the company makes sense to buy into at this point in time.

In my last article published about the company in July of this year, I mentioned that shares were getting quite pricey. I also pointed to both positive and negative catalysts for the company. In particular, there seems to be a lot of optimism revolving around the power of AI and how it can be applied to the firm's products and services. On the other hand, the business is facing competition in global markets, specifically competition from other smartphone manufacturers. Even though the stock was expensive, I made the case that it would be a better ‘hold’ than a ‘sell’ because of how high quality it is and because of its minimal amount of debt. Since then, the stock has pulled back somewhat, dipping by 1% at a time when the S&P 500 is up by 3.1%.

This spread is not terribly significant, especially over a three-month window of time. However, there is an opportunity for this spread to change rather meaningfully in the coming days. You see, after the market closes on October 31st, management will be announcing financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. Analysts are forecasting growth on both the top and bottom lines. If things can come in better than anticipated, shares might narrow that gap or even achieve upside greater than the broader market. But as somebody who does not like to speculate and after taking into consideration how the stock is priced, I believe that maintaining my ‘hold’ rating is logical right now.

Shares still don’t make sense to buy

When I last wrote about Apple a little over three months ago, investors had access to data covering through the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. But results now extend through the third quarter. During that most recent quarter, management achieved some rather impressive results. Revenue hit $85.78 billion. This is 4.9% above the $81.80 billion that the company reported one year earlier. For the most part, the company saw expansion on a product-by-product basis. As an example, Mac sales popped up by 2.5% from $6.84 billion to $7.01 billion. Even more impressive was the iPad, which saw an increase in revenue of 23.7% from $5.79 billion to $7.16 billion.

But to me, the most interesting part of the company would not be the products that it sells, but instead its services. This includes advertising revenue from its platforms, revenue associated with AppleCare, cloud services that it provides to customers, digital content sales, and certain payment services like Apple Card and Apple Pay. Speaking of digital content sales, this includes revenue associated with the firm’s App Store, as well as things like Apple Arcade, Apple Fitness+, Apple Music, Apple News+, and Apple TV+. I have always been a fan of service-oriented businesses. Their potential for recurring revenue, combined with their high margins, seem to be attractive in my book. And in the most recent quarter, this part of the company performed exceptionally well, with revenue of $24.21 billion. That's up 14.1% compared to the $21.21 billion the company reported one year earlier.

This is not to say that everything about the company is going well. It did see some weakness involving its wearables, home products, and accessories. These sales dropped by 2.3% from $8.28 billion to $8.10 billion. In addition to this, the company reported a decline in revenue involving its iPhone. This is, by far, the company's most significant product. Year over year, revenue dipped by 0.9% from $39.67 billion to $39.30 billion.

In an article that I published back in November of 2023, I talked about the market share that Apple has in the phone operating system space. When it comes to the US, it is the big giant out there. But outside of the US, focused on the global market, it is considerably smaller. Back then, for 2023, I calculated that it had a roughly 29% share of the global operating system industry. That did represent an increase over what it had been over the prior five years. However, it is becoming increasingly clear that the enterprise is facing continued pressure in key markets.

Unfortunately, management does not provide product by product revenue data on a geographic basis. But they do provide total revenue on a geographic basis. If you look at the image above, you can see financial performance for all of the major regions in which the firm operates. In every single one of these, with the exception of Greater China, the business experienced growth in the third quarter of 2024 compared to the same time last year. The same held true for the most part for the first nine months of 2024 compared to the same time of 2023. The one exception to this was the ‘Rest of Asia Pacific’ region where sales dipped by $9 million year over year. But while that might be a good chunk of money for you or I, it truly is a rounding error for Apple.

In Greater China, the company continues to struggle. In the most recent quarter, revenue of $14.73 billion was 6.5% below the $15.76 billion reported one year earlier. And for the first nine months of this year, revenue of $51.92 billion was 9.7% lower than the $57.48 billion the company reported the same time of 2023. Back in an article that I published about the behemoth in April of this year, I discussed the challenges that it was facing in China. There were regulatory issues, plus homegrown competition in the form of Huawei, that were causing the business issues.

As disappointing as all of this was, I did mention that the company still has plenty of growth opportunities elsewhere even if things don't go right in China. Specifically, I singled out India, which is expected to continue expanding from a population perspective even as China is destined to decline in the coming decades. With other parts of the world, including the US, expected to continue growing, I felt as though even the worst-case scenario would be little more than a slight pain for the company in the long run. I still feel that way today. Now obviously, this small problem could become a big one if it ever results in Apple losing its ability to manufacture its iPhones in China. After all, around 90% of these devices are produced there.

With revenue for the company increasing as of late, profits also improved. Net income grew from $19.88 billion to $21.45 billion. Operating cash flow popped from $26.38 billion to $28.86 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an increase from $25.63 billion to $27.17 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $26.05 billion to $28.20 billion. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of the 2024 fiscal year compared to the same time of 2023. Across the board, revenue, profits, and cash flows, are up. So even though the company is seeing weakness in one of its largest markets, it is still doing well enough elsewhere to more than compensate for this. It is worth noting that year over year results for the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year would actually be higher had it not been for the fact that the company had an extra operating week last year. So on a comparable basis, things are even better than they appear.

Throughout this article, you might get the impression that I am optimistic about the company. Operationally speaking, I certainly am. I firmly believe that Apple will be a larger business in 10 years than it is today. But this doesn't mean that I think the stock is worth buying into. If we annualize financial results that we have achieved so far this year, we would expect net profits for 2024 in its entirety to be $103.49 billion. Following the same approach with the other profitability metrics would result in adjusted operating cash flow of $123.59 billion and EBITDA of $129.49 billion.

In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company. That chart also includes valuation figures using results from 2023. Normally, these are the kinds of trading multiples that would convince me to rate a business a ‘sell’. But because we are talking about an industry leader, I believe that considering the company fairly valued would make more sense. Of course, others might disagree with me. After all, there are some things that the business has going for it that are appealing to investors.

For starters, over the last few years now, management has invested significant amounts of capital toward buying back stock. From the start of 2021 through the end of the third quarter of this year, Apple $322.79 billion toward share buybacks. This allowed the company to reduce its total share count by roughly 9%. Considering how the company is valued, I would personally prefer this capital be allocated toward growth. But considering also how large the business is, meaningful expansion might not be possible. I would also like to point out that the company has achieved this without increasing its overall debt. Net debt for the company actually dropped from $62.08 billion in 2021 to $39.50 billion in the most recent quarter. If we strip out long term marketable securities that are also on its books, then we actually go from having a slightly different situation of a net cash position of $65.80 billion in 2021 to $51.74 billion this year. So in that case, the situation did worsen some. But when you talk about exactly how large Apple truly is, I don't consider that to be a meaningful difference.

As always, I try to be open minded about situations changing. But those changes need to occur in response to legitimate changes to the fundamental health of the business in question. The next opportunity for that to arise will be on October 31st when, after the market closes, management will report financial results covering the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. The current expectation is for the company to report sales of $94.21 billion. Should this prove correct, it would imply a 5.3% increase in revenue over the $89.50 billion the company reported one year earlier. Earnings per share are also forecasted increase, rising from $1.46 to $1.59. Assuming we don't see a change in share count, this would translate to an improvement in net profits from $22.96 billion to $24.40 billion. In the table below, you can also see other profitability metrics for the final quarter of the 2023 fiscal year. If revenue and profits increase year over year, I can only imagine that these cash flow metrics will likely improve as well.

Takeaway

Believe me when I say that I that Apple is one of the best companies on the planet. It might not be growing at a rapid pace, but it is incredibly healthy. It generates a ton of cash flow and I believe in its long-term potential to continue expanding. Having said that, shares are pricey at this point in time and, even though its troubles in China won't make or break the business, those still do weigh on the company in the near term. Unless something changes when management reports results later this month, I do believe that keeping the firm rated a ‘hold’ makes sense right now

data.title: FNDB: I Love The Weightings
data.description:
I think it’s time investors consider market proxies that weight beyond market-cap. We are in a cycle where the S&P 500 and Nasdaq are dominated by idiosyncratic risk, whereby a select number of companies make up a huge amount of the weightings of those averages. If you agree, then you may want to consider the Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF (NYSEARCA:FNDB). This fund aims to follow the RAFI Fundamental High Liquidity US All Index. This fund stands out from typical index funds. Rather than basing company weights on market cap alone, FNDB uses a fundamental indexing method. This approach picks and weighs companies using key measures of their size. These include adjusted sales, retained operating cash flow, and dividends plus buybacks.

The fund has a clear goal: to deliver investment outcomes that mirror the total return of this index of U.S. companies, which are chosen based on fundamentals. Through this approach, FNDB seeks to give investors access to a wide range of the U.S. stock market, but with a focus on value and stocks that might yield higher dividends.

A Look At The Holdings

I love the weightings here.

Apple is the largest holding at 4.15%. The top 10 does NOT include Nvidia. And this is clearly well diversified. This is a far better reflection of “the market” than the S&P 500 as it’s diversified and based on fundamentals, not FOMO among a select number of mega-cap stocks while everything else has sub-par performance.

Sector and Global Allocation

FNDB's sector allocation gives us a look into its investment approach and possible risk-reward profile.

This breakdown of sectors shows a more even-handed approach when you compare it to indexes weighted by market cap, which often lean heavily toward tech. The big chunk given to financials and industrials hints at a value slant, which lines up with how fundamental indexing works.

Peer Comparison

When we compare FNDB to other funds, we need to look at regular market-cap weighted broad market ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Broad Market ETF (SCHB). FNDB stands out because it can do better in value-focused markets. When we look at the price ratio of FNDB to SCHB, we find that FNDB has underperformed since the Tech-driven rally began in 2023. But over the last 5 years it’s basically performed in-line. Personally, I like this.

Pros and Cons

The pro here? Fundamental indexing offers a different way to invest compared to market-cap weighting. FNDB looks at fundamentals instead of market cap alone. This means it might avoid the risks of putting money to work in overvalued stocks that can happen with cap-weighted indexes. The fund's focus on value stocks and companies with strong cash flows can help protect against downturns when markets get rough. FNDB gives broad market exposure and screens stocks based on fundamentals, striking a balance between active and passive management. This approach might attract investors who doubt pure market-cap weighting but aren't ready to jump into active strategies.

But we need to keep in mind the possible downsides. The fund's focus on value might cause it to underperform in growth-heavy markets, as we've seen with big tech stocks leading the way. Investors should be ready for times when it lags behind and have the grit to stick with the investment for the long haul if they believe cycles still exist.

Conclusion

The Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF offers a smart choice for investors who want something different from regular market-cap weighted index funds. Its fundamental indexing method, low fees (0.25%), and ability to potentially boost risk-adjusted returns make it worth thinking about for people who want to mix up their U.S. stock investments. I like the fund and think this isn’t a bad time to consider rotating out of other core market-cap weighting proxies in favor of this. In the event some of the mega-cap tech companies go through a significant correction, I suspect FNDB meaningfully outperforms. When you combine that scenario with the very real possibility of stock market breadth improving, helping to lift the other parts of the stock market which have lagged badly since 2021, the future looks potentially quite bright for this type of fundamental approach.

data.title: Home Depot: Valuation Is Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE:HD) operates as the largest home improvement retailer in the United States and other countries with a total of 2340 retail stores. With Home Depot’s fairly consistent growth and good capital efficiency, the stock has appreciated at a CAGR of 15.9% in the past decade. On top, Home Depot pays out a dividend with a current yield of 2.2%.

Yet, with a recent earnings hiccup due to a weaker macroeconomic backdrop, and an increasingly tight valuation, I believe that investors aren’t likely to see the great historic return to repeat.

Home Depot’s Earnings Have Grown Well & Capital Efficiently

Over the long term, Home Depot has expanded its retail network consistently while also growing same-store sales. As a result, the company’s revenue CAGR stands at a good 4.7% level from FY2001 to the current trailing revenues of $152.1 billion as of Q2/FY2024. The company also recently acquired SRS, a roofing, landscaping, and pool distributor, for $18.25 billion in June, bolstering future growth prospect.

The growth has also been achieved at a great return on total capital of 21.9%, and with a good trailing 13.9% operating margin, making Home Depot’s growth valuable to investors.

Ultimately, Home Depot’s growth story has still been similar to competitor Lowe’s Companies’ (LOW). Lowe’s has also grown revenues at quite a similar 4.3% CAGR from FY2014 to Q2/FY2024, having an even better 25.0% return on total capital but a slightly lower 12.5% operating margin. While leading the home improvement retailing industry in size, Home Depot isn’t unique as an investment in the industry.

Recent Macro-Driven Industry Hiccup Could Start to Subside

The good historical earnings growth has recently paused into moderate earnings declines after a peak in mid-2022. For example, in Q2, the company’s operating income still declined -0.8% year-on-year to $6.5 billion, and Home Depot guides for around a -1.8% total FY2024 operating income decline at the current guidance’s mid-point. With Home Depot’s relatively safe amount of debt and quite resistant earnings, the company is still positioned to weather further earnings declines.

Underneath, the macroeconomic backdrop has been weak as interest rates have risen and as the consumer sentiment has been weak. Existing home sales in the United States have also stayed at an incredibly low level, affecting home improvement product demand. Highlighting the weak industry, Lowe’s also downgraded the company’s guidance in August with similar earnings headwinds as Home Depot has reported. As Home Depot’s earnings declines are clearly macroeconomic in nature, I still believe that the long-term earnings outlook still stands good with an eventual uptick in the macroeconomic backdrop.

Positive trends in the industry could already start to be seen soon. As I wrote in my recent article on PulteGroup (PHM), focusing on the relatively weak short-term housing market but stronger long-term trends, lower mortgage rates have already started to have an impact on the housing market. Further, with a slowly recovering consumer sentiment and lowering inflation, improving purchasing power should start to transfer to Home Depot’s top and bottom line.

Also, while transitory in nature, the recent Hurricane Helene should aid home improvement retailers in upcoming quarters with a large amount of rebuilding, improving the short-term outlook. Home Depot should finally start to stabilize along with Lowe’s and others, and the relatively stable implied H2 guidance seems to already suggest starting stabilization.

Home Depot still sees macroeconomic uncertainty ahead as discussed in the Q2 earnings call, highlighting that the short-term outlook is still uncertain despite some optimistic macroeconomic metrics for the company.

Home Depot’s Valuation Is Too Tight

I constructed a discounted cash flow [DCF] model for the HD stock to estimate a rough fair value.

In the DCF model, I estimate Home Depot to return to fairly good growth in FY2025 with a 5.5% growth, slowing down gradually to 2.5% perpetual growth. The model considers fairly well-continuing long-term growth at an estimated revenue CAGR of 3.8% from FY2023 to FY2033.

The growth should also aid Home Depot’s margins back up slightly, and I estimate the EBIT margin to return to 14.5% from an estimated 13.6% margin in FY2024. With Home Depot’s good working capital management and moderate capital expenditures, I estimate the company’s cash flow conversion to be good in the future as well.

The estimates put Home Depot’s fair value estimate at $314.52, 24% below the stock price at the time of writing. The stock’s current valuation seems to price in growth above a historical performance, making for a likely weaker future return and a weak risk-to-reward.

Also, despite a lower return on capital and quite similar growth, Home Depot’s forward 19.1 EV/EBITDA exceeds Lowe’s 16.1 forward EV/EBITDA by a considerable margin. While I believe Home Depot to return to relatively good and valuable growth, I believe that better alternatives exist in the market due to the steep valuation.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a weighted average cost of capital of 7.67%. The used WACC is derived from a capital asset pricing model:

In Q2, Home Depot had $573 million in interest expenses, making the company’s interest rate 4.30% with the current amount of interest-bearing debt. I estimate a moderate 15% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

To estimate the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.08% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.11% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s estimate for the US, updated in July. Yahoo Finance estimates Home Depot’s beta at 1.01. With a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity stands at 8.33% and the WACC at 7.67%.

Takeaway

Home Depot’s good long-term growth has been disturbed by a weak macroeconomic backdrop, also seen throughout the housing market and with competitor Lowe’s earnings. The industry’s outlook should slowly start to improve with lower interest rates, improving consumer sentiment, and lower inflation, and also aided in the short term by Hurricane Helene. Yet, the short-term outlook stands more uncertain.

Despite the recent earnings hiccup, investors are pricing Home Depot’s stock at an increasing valuation ahead of an earnings recovery. As the stock now seems priced for too ambitious earnings growth, I initiate Home Depot at a Sell rating despite the company’s long-term success.

data.title: Nebius Group: A Positive View Before The Resumption Of Share Trading
data.description:
Background

Nebius Group (NASDAQ:NBIS) published a press release on Thursday announcing that its stock will resume trading on NASDAQ on Monday, October 21st. On Friday, the company released an updated presentation of its business. As a result, I have updated my valuation of the company. As a reminder, as part of my previous article, I noted that the company's fair valuation was at $4.7 billion ($23.6 per share), excluding the possible positive effect on the company's fair value of the share buyback that the company's shareholders agreed to in August. In this article, I will share my updated valuation of the company based on the newly published data. It is important to note that my view of the company's prospects has not changed, although the final valuation has declined slightly. As before, I suggest that the best approach to Nebius Group's valuation is a sum-of-the-parts approach. Now let's get to the details of the update.

Nebius AI

Addressable Market

The core and main part of NBIS's business is Nebius AI. The addressable market for this company is infrastructure for the development of artificial intelligence (GPU-as-a-service and AI Cloud). According to the presentation, the company expects the addressable market for Nebius AI to grow to $260 billion by 2030 (CAGR of 35%) from $33 billion in 2023. The main driver of growth is increasing demand for inference of LLMs. According to Gartner, in 2023, 66% of customer spending on AI servers was on training LLMs and 34% on inference. Gartner expects that by 2027 64% of AI server spending will be on inference and 36% on training.

It's worth noting here that NBIS' estimate of the addressable market is higher than in previous presentations and reports. But this is more likely due to the addition of new segments (Platform-as-a-service) to the Nebius AI business, whereas earlier the company referred only to the GPU-as-a-Service market estimate and third-party industry reports, but now it cites internal estimates. Perhaps the company's internal calculations have shown that the prospects for the GPU-as-a-Service market are higher than Fortune Business Insights expected ($50 billion by 2032). Nevertheless, it is very difficult to predict 2030, but what is certain is that the demand for GPU computing rentals is huge now, so the market should be enough for everyone. The only question is which companies will have sufficient access to capital and Nvidia's GPU supply.

Customers

The company already has more than 40 customers, up from only 10 at the end of 2023. Let's briefly break down the ones that were cited in the presentation:

  • Mistral AI is a French startup founded in 2023 known for its open-source large language models. Its last round valuation in June 2024 (post-money) was $6.5 billion (according to Pitchbook).The startup has raised a total of $1.2 billion. Slide 31 of the presentation indicates that Mistral AI uses Nebius' cluster of thousands of GPUs
  • JetBrains - is a global software company founded in 2020 that specializes in creating intelligent, productivity-enhancing tools for software developers and teams. I haven't found any data on its valuation in public sources. It uses Nebius' cluster of hundreds of H100 GPUs.
  • Genesis Therapeutics is a biotechnology company founded in 2019 with a focus on the developing drugs using artificial intelligence. Last round valuation in August 2023 was at $424 million.
  • Recraft is a developer of a platform for generating and editing vector graphics and 3D images. It was founded in 2022. The company raised $12 million in January, but the round’s valuation was not disclosed. According to the presentation, Nebius is the main supplier for the inference for Recraft's text-to-image 20B model.
  • Higgsfield is a 2023-founded startup with a focus on developing artificial intelligence technology for video content creation. The company was valued at $30 million (post-money) in a seed round in April 2024.

Plans for data centers development

Nebius' key investments are now going into data center expansion in Finland (Mantsala). Nebius plans to triple its power capacity to 75 MW and the number of GPUs in it to 60 thousand by the end of 2025 or the beginning of 2026. In 2Q24, the first phase of the Finnish data center expansion began.

In July 2024, the company signed an agreement with data center operator Equinix to colocate a part of the data center in Paris. It is expected to be launched at the end of November. According to the company, this will increase the current company's GPU capacity by 25%. It also plans to add more data centers in different geographies, including the United States. On slide 15 of the presentation it is indicated that the launch of a data center in the USA is coming soon.

When it comes to the number of GPUs, Nebius expects to have the computing equivalent of 20 thousand Nvidia H100s by the end of 2024. For 2025, the company gives a range of expectations for the number of GPUs from 35 thousand to 60+ thousand Nvidia H100 equivalent. And in the medium term - from 60 and up to 240 thousand Nvidia H100 equivalent. This strong variation is due to uncertainty about how much capital will be available for expansion during the respective periods.

Financials

Below is a graph from the company's presentation showing the dynamics of revenue ARR (annualized run-rate). This rapid growth was due to the increase in data center capacity from 2 thousand in 4Q23 to 20+ thousand GPUs at the end of 2024 and increased demand from customers, which number has grown from 10 in 2023 to 40+ currently.

The company expects revenue in 2024 to be in the range of $70-80 million, with an adjusted EBITDA loss of $60-70 million and capital expenditures of $0.9-1 billion. For Q3, the company posted $164 million in capital expenditures. It is worth noting that the company lowered its revenue guidance for the year-end ARR. In the company's previous presentations, the company expected year-end revenue ARR of $200 million, and I noted in my last article that I expect the company to beat that forecast. From the above, it appears that the company has lowered the forecast. I do not see it as critical to business development, as the company continues to grow rapidly.

For the next year, the company expects year-end ARR of $500-1000 million and revenue of $400-600 million. The company also expects positive adjusted EBITDA for Nebius AI for the next year. However, I will note that free cash flow will be negative as capital expenditures will be in the range of $600 to $1500 million. These will go towards the purchase of Nvidia GB200 GPUs and data center expansion/additions.

Valuation

In my previous article, I concluded that the valuation of Nebius AI was $1.28 billion, or $6.43 per share. At that time, I used the following methodology to determine Nebius' final valuation.

First, I took the then-current valuation of comparable bigtech companies (with cloud business) at 7.4x 2024 revenue and multiplied that by an expected year-end ARR of $200 million. Right now, bigtech with cloud businesses trades at an average of 7.1x 2024 revenue and 6.1x 2025 revenue. Multiplying that by the average year-end ARR management expectation of $180 million and the average 2025 revenue expectation of $500 million, we get valuations of $1.28 billion and $3.05 billion. The spread is quite large. I'll assume that $1.28 billion is the lower end of Nebius AI's valuation, and we'll stick with it for conservatism (previously it was $1.48 billion).

The second approach in last week's article was a valuation based on the valuation of such private companies as Coreweave and Lambda Labs. At that time, I used average last round valuation for these companies divided by Sacra's expected 2024 revenue. The average EV/S'24 multiple on them back then was 5.4x. What's changed? Since then, CoreWeave has been reported to have a secondary round with an estimated $23 billion valuation. CoreWeave's 2024 revenue, as previously, is expected to be $2.4 billion, according to Pitchbook. Lambda Labs had a new round in August. The company raised $800 million, but the valuation was not disclosed. According to Notice, its valuation is now at $2.2 billion. Lambda Labs's (Pitchbook) revenue expectations are unchanged at $600 million. This makes the average valuation of these companies of 6.6x 2024 revenue. Multiplying that by an ARR of $180 million yields a valuation of $1.19 billion (last time it was $1.08 billion).

As a result, for the Nebius AI valuation, I took the average of these two valuations - $1.24 billion (previously $1.28 billion), which, taking into account the 199 million outstanding shares of Nebius Group, gives us a valuation of $6.2 per share.

Let's move on to the valuation of other companies of the group, but in a more condensed form.

Toloka AI

Business

To recap, the company is in the business of providing high-quality data to train artificial intelligence models. The company wants to become the Uber of the data world for artificial intelligence, that is, connecting those who need to annotate data with those who can and want to annotate that data for a fee.

The company has a subsidiary Mindrift for data annotation, through which Toloka AI is marking up all sorts of data with the help of over 8,000 experts. In comparison, the current world leader in data annotation, Scale AI, has a network of 240,000 freelancers who are engaged in data annotation.

According to the presentation, Toloka AI expects the expert data market for generative artificial intelligence to grow from $2 billion in 2024 to $17 billion in 2030 (a CAGR of 40%). Toloka AI's clients include Microsoft, ServiceNow, Hugging Face, and other major technology companies.

Financials and valuation

The company does not disclose its financials. However, potential 2025 revenue for this segment can be calculated as follows. For Nebius Group as a whole, management expects $500-700 million in revenue. From this, I subtract management's expectations of $400-600 million in revenue for Nebius AI and get $100 million in revenue for the 3 remaining businesses (Toloka AI, TripleTen and Avride). Since Avride will have minimal revenue (even though it has a partnership with Uber), that $100 million will be split between Toloka AI and TripleTen. I expect it to be split in the approximate proportion of $60 million (Toloka AI) and $40 million (TripleTen), based on TripleTen's 3Q24 ARR of $27.2 million.

Last time, I took a rough estimate on private companies Scale AI (the largest player in the data annotation market) and LabelBox as the basis for my valuation. Now I have expanded the list of closest peers for comparison and note that this is a highly competitive market. There is a publicly traded approximate peer, Appen Limited (OTCPK:APPEF). Its stock is trading at 1.3x 2025 revenue, but it's worth noting that consensus expects Appen's 2024 revenue to decline 18% YoY but grow 7% YoY in 2025. Given Toloka AI's faster-growing business, a higher multiple should be applied to its valuation, so let's determine it based on non-public peers.

Scale AI last round was valued at $14 billion with revenue expectations (Pitchbook) for 2024 of $1.4 billion. Applying this multiple to my Toloka AI revenue assumption of $60 million, we get a maximum Toloka AI valuation of $600 million. For Labelbox and Snorkel AI, the last rounds were in 2021 and they are now unrepresentative, so let's turn to Notice. On Notice, Labelbox's valuation is now approximately $581 million and Snorkel AI's valuation is $413 million.

Last time I stopped at Toloka AI's valuation of $500 million ($2.5 per share of Nebius Group), I will probably keep it at this level, as I don't see much reason to change it.

TripleTen

TripleTen is an edtech platform specializing in reskilling and upskilling individuals for careers in tech. It currently offers five study tracks - Software Engineering, Data Science, Business Intelligence Analytics, Quality Assurance, and Cybersecurity.

The addressable market for global online education is estimated at $19 billion by 2024 and is expected to reach $95 billion by 2030 (CAGR 26%). However, there are a lot of players in this market and overall online education after 2020-2021 is facing challenges, especially in terms of valuation (with the exception of Duolingo (DUOL)). Last time, I estimated a $221 million valuation for TripleTen. I used an average multiple between Duolingo (DUOL), Udemy (UDMY) and Coursera (COUR) of 3.7x 2024 revenues and the company's ARR bookings forecast of 80 million at the end of 2024 to value this business. Since then, the average EV/S'24 multiple for these companies has grown to 5.8x (due to the strong growth in Duolingo's valuation), and the EV/S'25 multiple on them is now 4.65x. Multiplying that by my expected TripleTen 2025 revenue of $40 million, I got a valuation for TripleTen of $186 million or $0.93 per share.

Avride

In the previous article, I estimated a valuation of $109.8 million for Avride. At that time, I calculated its valuation based on the valuations of comparable non-public companies (Waymo and Starship Technologies), adjusting it in proportion to the number of passenger rides in autonomous taxis and the number of food deliveries by robot couriers. Since then, the company has announced a partnership with Uber (UBER), which clearly adds value to Avride. Given the uncertainty regarding Avride's revenue growth for the coming years, I believe it's reasonable to consider the cost of achieving current results as a minimum fair valuation for Avride, especially since the company has disclosed it. According to page 82 of the presentation, from 2017 through October 2024, the company invested $310 million to develop its autonomous cars and robots. The company noted that this is far less than Waymo, Cruise, and Zoox have invested in growing their businesses. In my view, Avride's fair value is at least $310 million, and in reality, it is likely to be closer to $1 billion or higher, but for now, I will estimate it at $310 million or $1.56 per share of Nebius Group.

Equity stake in other companies

Nebius Group previously disclosed a stake in ClickHouse, but the exact numbers were not provided. Now, the company has indicated that Nebius Group owns approximately 28% of ClickHouse. Recall that ClickHouse was once a part of Yandex N.V., but it spun off from it in 2021. ClickHouse's last known valuation was in 2021. At that time, the company was valued at $2 billion as part of a Series B financing. It is important to note that 2021 saw high valuations for software companies in funding rounds. The valuations of many public and private companies in the software segment have dropped a lot since then. It's hard to say whether the company's financial indicators have increased enough to overwhelm the overall decline in multiples, so for the sake of conservatism, let's assume ClickHouse's valuation has declined since then. According to the Notice data, it is around $602 million. Thus, the valuation of Nebius' 28% stake in ClickHouse amounts to $168 million. As a part of the previous article, I used a book value of $0.1 billion as the valuation of all Nebius' stakes in other companies. I revalued this part at the new ClickHouse valuation of $0.169 billion.

Final thoughts on valuation

Summing the values of all the businesses ($1.24 billion + $0.5 billion + $0.186 billion + $0.31 billion) results in a valuation of Nebius Group's operating business of $2.24 billion, or $11.23 per share (previously $2.11 billion, or $10.6 per share). Add to that the valuation of the ClickHouse stake at $0.169 billion and we get $2.4 billion or $12.1 per share. Next, the liquidity cushion should be added. At the end of 2Q24, the company had a cash balance of $2.33 billion. Slide 8 of the presentation shows that as of September 30, the company had a total cash position of $2.3 billion. However, the company could raise debt during the 3Q24 quarter. Let's define the size of the net cash position at the end of 3Q24 as $2.33 billion (2Q24 cash and cash equivalents balance) minus 3Q2024 CAPEX of $164 million minus Q3'2024 Adj EBITDA loss of $7 million and minus debt of $10 million. This results in an estimate of net cash at the end of 3Q24 of $2.15 billion. Below is a table showing the change in my valuation of Nebius Group (individual businesses and the company as a whole) since the previous article.

Part of Nebius Group's Valuation Old estimate, bln $ New estimate, bln $
Nebius AI 1.24 1.28
Toloka AI 0.5 0.5
TripleTen 0.221 0.186
Avride 0.11 0.31
Equity in other companies 0.1 0.169
Net cash position 2.5 2.15
Total 4.7 4.6

Source: created by author using his estimates

Thus, I believe Nebius Group's valuation (equity value) is now $4.6 billion or $23 per share (previously $23.6 per share). I am waiting for the company's stock to resume trading on NASDAQ on Monday and do not plan to sell my shares after the resumption of trading. I expect extremely high volatility in the company's stock price in the first days of trading, given the fact that Nebius' shares has not been traded for 2.5 years and the free float is 78.1% (slide 95 of the presentation). If NBIS' share price falls below $10 per share after trading resumes, I intend to increase my position. I plan to publish the next update on the company's investment case a few days after the 3Q24 (October 31) financials are released.

data.title: BLOK: Crypto Stocks Break Out Ahead Of Election (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The next two weeks will be about the “Trump trade” and the “Harris trade.” Both candidates are thought to be better for cryptocurrency than the current POTUS and sitting SEC chair. It’s clear, however, that the former President has embraced the bitcoin crowd more than the current vice president.

That claim is born out in price action. Since mid-September, Trump has gained ground in both the polls and in election betting markets. At the same time, crypto has been in rally mode, with significant gains also being had in blockchain stocks.

I am upgrading the Amplify Transformational Data Sharing ETF (NYSEARCA:BLOK) from a hold to a buy. I was cautious on the fund back in March, anticipating some consolidation after the bitcoin halving event. That largely played out with BLOK falling from the mid-$30s to, at times, under $30 in Q2 and Q3.

Now, with a series of higher highs and higher lows off the Tokyo crash nadir, the fund is at the best level since January 2022 while the valuation is not overly high.

Trump With A Slight Betting-Market Advantage

According to the issuer, BLOK invests at least 80% of its net assets in the equity securities of companies actively involved in the development and utilization of blockchain technologies. Blockchain is a peer-to-peer shared, distributed ledger that can track tangible, intangible, and digital assets, and companies in all business sectors through the process of recording transactions and tracking assets in a business network.

BLOK remains a small ETF with just $747 million in assets under management (about unchanged since Q1) while its annual expense ratio is high at 0.76% as of October 18, 2024. Share-price momentum is stellar with an A+ ETF grade from Seeking Alpha’s quantitative ETF grading system, but the dividend yield is very low at 0.84%.

BLOK is also considered a risky ETF due to its concentration and high historical annualized standard deviation trends. Liquidity can be a weak spot too given average daily trading volume of just 140,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of 13 basis points, per Amplify ETFs. So I encourage investors to use limit orders during the trading day.

Looking closer at the portfolio, BLOK’s allocation has taken a positive turn in the sense that there are more profitable companies now compared to my previous analysis back in the first quarter of this year. In net, Morningstar reports that the price-to-earnings ratio is just shy of 20 while the price-to-sales ratio is not overly expensive at just 2.8.

With a long-term EPS growth rate of 9.8%, the resulting PEG ratio is significantly more attractive today compared to earlier this year. From a risk point of view, BLOK has ample diversification across the style box, though there is a growth bent. A high percentage of domestic SMID caps adds to cyclical risk too.

BLOK: Portfolio Breakdown & Valuation Metrics

While there is nuance to the valuation and factor allocation breakdown, the sector mix is straightforward. BLOK is a fintech ETF – more than half of the ETF is invested in the Financials sector while one-third of BLOK is allocated to the Information Technology sector.

There are low holdings in other sectors. The top 10 holdings account for a significant portion of the total portfolio – 39%.

BLOK: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, there isn’t a long track record off of which to form a trend thesis in this respect, but there is some data to analyze. The October through February stretch has historically been strong, though December has been volatile and mixed in total returns.

Prospective investors should allocate no more than a small portion of their portfolios to this fund given very high month-by-month swings in price.

BLOK: Broadly Bullish Seasonal Trends Through February

The Technical Take

With a decent valuation and generally favorable seasonals, BLOK’s technical chart looks strong. Notice in the graph below that shares are in a broad uptrend and broke out to a 52-week high last week. I see resistance at an uptrend line that comes into play near $49 – that's also a key downside gap from early 2022, so we can have some confluence there.

On the downside, a support line is currently near $32, so that would be a large amount of risk. It’s possible that a closer support zone may be the $36 to $37 range – the peak from right after my previous analysis in March and April.

With a rising long-term 200-day moving average, the bulls control the primary trend. I am also encouraged by a very strong RSI momentum reading at the top of the chart, helping to confirm the upside price thrust. Finally, there are simply very few shares traded from last week’s closing level up to about $50, so another 20% of upside could come quickly in my technical opinion.

BLOK: Bullish Upside Breakout, $49 Price Target, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on BLOK. While crypto stocks are very risky and volatile, the fund’s valuation appears better today while the technical chart shows an uptrend ahead of the US election.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500: Signs Of Weakness, Week Starting 21st October (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPY) furthered its rally to 5878 and last week's call for a pre-election correction looks on shaky ground. After all, it appears Trump has taken a lead in the polls and without election uncertainty, stocks might not correct. That's one theory, anyway. The other, and one that I will stick with for now, is that the conditions for a correction are still present, and at last there are signs of weakness in the price action. Election uncertainty or not, there could still be a correction.

This week's article points out some clues that a reversal may be developing, and outlines where the uptrend needs to hold. Various techniques will be applied to multiple timeframes in a top-down process which also considers the major market drivers. The aim is to provide an actionable guide with directional bias, important levels, and expectations for future price action.

S&P 500 Monthly

The usual negative seasonality trends for September and October in election years haven't played out so far. However, there are 9 sessions to go until month end and a drop past the 5757 open into a lower close is still quite possible.

The monthly chart below is on the logarithmic scale to highlight the Fibonacci extension of the 2022 bear market at 5879. It's a level I've been highlighting for nearly two months, and the most important position in the vicinity. A break through would target the big 6000, but I think is more likely a Christmas present for bulls than something which can be achieved before the election.

Targets are unchanged from previous weeks and there are only Fibonacci extensions and measured moves to guide us. 5879 is the 161.8% Fibonacci extension of the 2022 bear market on the logarithmic chart.

5767 is the September high and the first support. 5669-74 at the July high and October bottom is a major inflection.

The August bar completed the upside Demark exhaustion count and may still have an effect by hampering progress.

S&P 500 Weekly

A bullish weekly bar formed this week. It's quite similar to the bars which formed following the complete weekly Demark exhaustion counts in July '23 and July '24 (all complete counts highlighted below). Both topped the next week.

There are no further Fib extensions or measured moves until above 6000.

Minor support is 5804 and 5767. Major support is now 5669-74. There's not much below that until 5400, although the rising 20-week MA may be worth noting around 5600.

S&P 500 Daily

Monday's new high was in-line with last week's expectations and was immediately reversed by Tuesday's drop. However, the real reversal came on Thursday with a gap to a new high at 5878 followed by a fall into a weak close. This clearly indicated a rejection at new highs. It also came on bar 8 of a Demark exhaustion count and Friday completed the signal.

The below chart highlights some similarities with the previous topping pattern in July. In fact, the whole rally from the September low has been similar. Whether it reverses in the same way remains to be seen, but the comparison does suggest behaviour and positioning is starting to shift.

Targets come from the monthly/weekly references. There is no real resistance.

5804 is a weak low, but may line up with the rising trendline and 20dma on Tuesday/Wednesday so could be significant. Breaking through would lead to 5767 and likely lower.

A Demark exhaustion count completed on Friday. Combined with the weekly and monthly signals in play, there are decent odds for a reaction (dip/pause).

Drivers/Events

Earnings season and rotations helped the S&P500 this week. There was mixed news on semiconductors: rumors of export restrictions and ASML earnings weighed, while Taiwan Semiconductors (TSM) earnings impressed. Some "Mag 7" stocks such as Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL) managed to make new all-time highs but could not hold them.

Data was mostly overlooked, and any weak spots can be attributed to the hurricane. It would take something significant to change the Fed's set course of two more 25bps cuts this year, so there may not be much movement on economic news unless it is really shocking. There are some concerns building over inflation creeping back, so this is something to monitor.

Stocks, the US dollar and Bitcoin all got an early election boost from better polls for Trump. These things can swing either way, however, and election uncertainty is by no means in the rear mirror.

Next week's scheduled events are on the light side. PMIs and Unemployment Claims on Thursday aren't likely to lead to any sustained moves. Tesla (TSLA) earnings on Wednesday are the main event.

Probable Moves Next Week(s)

Last week, I pointed out various conditions which made a reversal more likely. However, there wasn't any confirmation from the actual price action which remained strong. This week, there has been some weaker action, at least on the daily chart, and this suggests a reversal may be developing. Both the weekly and daily charts have similarities to the July top and a pre-election correction is still my call.

The 5804 low is key to the bullish/bearish bias for next week as it lines up with the trendline and 20dma. 5767 is the next downside level, but a dip this far would have already compromised the trend, so I'd expect it to eventually break for the long-awaited pullback.

If there is no reversal next week, I'd have to concede the signals have not worked and my view is wrong. I am longer-term bullish into next year, so I'll may have to find ways to rejoin the trend, and I will outline these next week. I'm hoping I don't have to!

data.title: Etsy: A Pandemic Growth Stock Ready For A Rebound In 2025
data.description:
Thesis

E-commerce platform Etsy (Nasdaq:ETSY) was undoubtedly one of the biggest winners during the Covid-19 pandemic. With people confined to their homes and unable to spend on experiences or travel, many resorted to online shopping. Flush with stimulus checks, consumers flooded Etsy with purchases, doubling its marketplace size almost overnight. However, this heyday didn’t last forever. As the macro environment deteriorated and consumers tightened their wallets, Etsy began to crack.

Fast forward to today, investors have written Etsy off for dead, with shares down over 80% from their 2021 peak. Looking at the stock chart, you would expect the company to be permanently impaired and on its way out. However, this couldn’t be farther from the truth. Trading at $51 per share, Etsy has returned to price levels seen in 2018. The difference? Revenues are up 4.5x, margins have expanded, and earnings have increased 4x.

So why does Mr. Market continue to punish Etsy? As is often the case, markets tend to overreact in both directions, swinging from one extreme to another without settling on a middle ground. Was Etsy overvalued during the pandemic at 60x earnings? Absolutely. However, trading at 11x earnings today, levels typically reserved for Utilities, is wildly exaggerated.

As consumer discretionary spending picks up and sentiment gains momentum, I expect Mr. Market to change his tune. Management has a variety of levers to pull, including a new loyalty program and Gift mode, which should serve as key catalysts for kickstarting growth. Once Etsy demonstrates that it can grow gross merchandise sales (GMS), its primary metric, at a mid-single-digit clip, I expect a multiple re-rating closer to the sector median of 18x. In the meantime, the company’s highly attractive, asset-light business model and fortress balance sheet will enable management to continue heavily repurchasing shares at this incredibly cheap valuation.

With the holiday season right around the corner and expectations at their lowest, even the slightest positive developments could spark a rally in shares. Not to mention, with nearly 15% short interest, such a rally could result in a massive short squeeze. Rather than sitting on the sidelines, I see the current valuation as a “pound the table” buying opportunity for long-term investors and have started aggressively adding shares at these levels.

Company Background

Founded in 2005, Etsy is a two-sided online marketplace focused on selling handmade or vintage items and craft supplies. When I think of Etsy, I picture things like custom jewelry, engraved cutting boards, and handmade candles.

In FY2023, Etsy generated $13.2 billion in gross merchandise sales (GMS), a decrease of 1.2% year-over-year. The breakdown of GMS included Home and Living at 35% ($4.6 billion), Jewelry and Accessories at 20% ($2.6 billion), Apparel at 15% ($2 billion), Craft Supplies at 10% ($1.3 billion), Paper and Party Supplies at 5% ($600 million), and Toys and Games at 5% ($600 million). While the core Etsy marketplace accounts for nearly 90% of GMS, the company also owns Reverb, a musical instrument marketplace, and Depop, a fashion resale platform, accounting for 7.1% and 4.6%, respectively. With nearly 92 million active buyers, 9 million active sellers, and over 120 million items for sale, Etsy ranks as the third most popular e-commerce site in the U.S.

Etsy has one of the most unique, attractive business models I've encountered in my research. The asset-light structure requires little to no capital expenditures, as there are no warehouses or inventories. Instead, Etsy simply acts as a facilitator between sellers and buyers, earning fees for its services. The company's consolidated take rate (essentially Etsy's share for every dollar spent) is 22%. Over 70% of Etsy's revenues come directly from the marketplace, which includes transaction fees, payment processing fees, and listing fees. The remaining 27.3% is generated from optional services such as on-site advertising and shipping labels.

The cost of revenues really only consists of payment processing and cloud infrastructure fees, resulting in gross margins north of 70% and adjusted EBITDA margins of around 28%. It doesn't get much better than that, folks.

Moving to management, CEO Josh Silverman is certainly a jockey worth betting on. Silverman is well-known for his turnaround capabilities, having held top positions at eBay (NASDAQ:EBAY) and previously serving as CEO of Skype, where he attracted 300 million new users, doubled revenues, and tripled profits during his tenure. His appointment as CEO of Etsy marked a significant inflection point for the company, which was fending off activists and facing potential acquisition by private equity as it struggled to manage costs. Within months of taking over in 2017, Silverman obsessed with cutting costs, laying off nearly a quarter of the workforce, and rapidly transforming the company's financial model.

My only knock on management is that I would like to see more insider transactions at these depressed prices. In the last twelve months, there have been 47 insider sales with zero open market purchases and only 1% of shares held by insiders. Other than that, this is as sound of a management team as it comes.

Etsy Post Pandemic

As I mentioned earlier, Etsy's stock performance during the pandemic makes total sense. E-commerce boomed, jumping 55% during COVID-19, as consumers found themselves flush with stimulus cash and had virtually nothing else to spend it on. If you look at the stock chart, Etsy is a near mirror image of another pandemic fallen angel, Paypal (NASDAQ:PYPL), which I'm also heavily invested in. The point is that these conditions were an anomaly.
